lesson.title: Determinants
lesson.body: The determinant is a scalar function of a square matrix that encodes geometric and algebraic properties. For a 2×2 matrix, det([[a,b],[c,d]]) = ad − bc. For larger matrices, cofactor expansion along any row or column works. Key properties: det(AB) = det(A)·det(B), det(Aᵀ) = det(A), and a matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is nonzero.

rooms.question: If det(A) = 5 and det(B) = 3, what is det(AB)?
rooms.explanation: det(AB) = det(A)·det(B) = 5·3 = 15. This multiplicative property is a key theorem.

rooms.question: What happens to det(A) when a row is multiplied by scalar c?
rooms.explanation: Scaling one row by c scales the determinant by c. Scaling all n rows (equivalently, multiplying the full matrix by c) scales det by cⁿ.

rooms.question: Order the cofactor expansion steps to compute det of a 3×3 matrix along row 1:
items: Identify the three entries a₁₁, a₁₂, a₁₃ in row 1
Compute the 2×2 minors M₁₁, M₁₂, M₁₃
rooms.explanation: Cofactor expansion: select a row, compute the 2×2 minor for each entry, apply the checkerboard sign pattern, then sum.

rooms.question: A square matrix A is invertible (non-singular) if and only if:
rooms.explanation: Invertibility is equivalent to det(A) ≠ 0. A singular matrix has det = 0 and no inverse.

boss.name: The Singular Matrix
boss.taunt: My determinant is zero. You cannot invert me. You cannot solve me.

questions.question: det(A⁻¹) = ?
questions.explanation: Since AA⁻¹ = I and det(I)=1: det(A)·det(A⁻¹) = 1, so det(A⁻¹) = 1/det(A).

questions.question: For what value of k does the matrix [[2, k], [1, 3]] become singular?
questions.explanation: det = 2·3 − k·1 = 6 − k. Set to zero: k = 6.

questions.question: The determinant of an upper triangular matrix is:
questions.explanation: For triangular matrices (upper or lower), the determinant equals the product of diagonal entries. This makes row reduction efficient for computing determinants.
